audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Exodus / Shemot text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for first Pharaoh confrontation, wilderness service request, intensified brick labor, Israelite foremen, and Moses' lament.

verses.restored: Afterward Moses and Aaron came and said to Pharaoh, Thus says YHWH, Divine of Israel: Send my people away, so they may hold festival to me in the wilderness.
Pharaoh said, Who is YHWH, that I should listen to his voice to send Israel away? I do not know YHWH, and also Israel I will not send.
They said, Divine of the Hebrews has met with us. Please let us go a three-day journey in wilderness and sacrifice to YHWH our Divine, lest he strike us with pestilence or sword.
The king of Egypt said to them, Moses and Aaron, why do you loosen the people from their works? Go to your burdens.
Pharaoh said, Look, the people of the land are now many, and you would make them rest from their burdens.
That day Pharaoh commanded the taskmasters over the people and their foremen, saying,
You shall no longer give straw to the people to make bricks as yesterday and before; let them go and gather straw for themselves.

But the quota of bricks that they made yesterday and before you shall place upon them; you shall not reduce from it, for they are idle. Therefore they cry out, saying, Let us go, let us sacrifice to our Divine.
Let the service be heavy upon the men, and let them work at it, and let them not pay attention to words of falsehood.
The taskmasters of the people and their foremen went out and said to the people, Thus says Pharaoh: I am not giving you straw.
You yourselves go, get straw for yourselves wherever you may find it, but none of your service will be reduced.
So the people scattered through all land of Egypt to gather stubble for straw.
The taskmasters pressed, saying, Complete your works, the matter of a day in its day, as when there was straw.

The foremen of the children of Israel, whom Pharaoh's taskmasters had set over them, were beaten, saying, Why have you not completed your prescribed amount of bricks yesterday and today, as yesterday and before?
The foremen of the children of Israel came and cried to Pharaoh, saying, Why do you do thus to your servants?
Straw is not given to your servants, and they say to us, Make bricks. Look, your servants are beaten, but fault is with your people.
He said, Idle, you are idle. Therefore you say, Let us go sacrifice to YHWH.
Now go, serve; straw will not be given to you, but the quota of bricks you shall give.
The foremen of the children of Israel saw themselves in harm, saying, You shall not reduce from your bricks, the matter of a day in its day.
They met Moses and Aaron standing to meet them as they came out from Pharaoh.
They said to them, May YHWH see you and judge, because you made our smell stink in eyes of Pharaoh and in eyes of his servants, to give sword in their hand to kill us.
Moses returned to YHWH and said, My Lord, why have you brought harm to this people? Why then did you send me?
Since I came to Pharaoh to speak in your name, he has done harm to this people, and rescuing, you have not rescued your people.

translation_notes: Pharaoh responds to liberation-worship by intensifying avodah/service into heavier bondage.
Rest from burdens is treated by Pharaoh as threat; Sabbath/liberation themes are already implied.
Moses' lament is preserved honestly; the first act of obedience makes oppression worse before deliverance unfolds.
safety_note: The chapter exposes how oppressive power reacts to liberation demands by increasing workload and blaming the oppressed. Do not spiritualize this away from material labor conditions.
